difference between Riley's rays and monsell value is that Riley's grades were evenly visually spaced between black and white paint so monsell and developing a system wanted it to be universally applicable the whole idea is that any color in any material produced would be able to be described within his system so that's why he chose an absolute or theoretical black and white Point whereas Riley was only concerned with paint he was only concerned with being able to take the color and value range that we see in the world which is very very Broad and compressing it down to what can be expressed with paint and in paint you can't have anything darker than black paint or anything lighter than white paint so the Riley values do differ slightly from muscle values especially at the lower end of the scale towards the center there's a fairly close correspondence but for instance Riley's darkest values are slightly lighter than the monsell this becomes relevant because of how Riley applied value to his system Riley used four basic lighting conditions and for each of these lighting conditions he devised value scales for basic upright planes um so essentially if you had something like a cube um you could determine what the the what the range between light and Shadow would be on a white Cube and on a black Cube and then the other values would be spaced evenly between them this does not correspond precisely to how Vision Works because there is compression in the dark um but in terms of visual impact and storytelling it was certainly close enough form lighting was the most common lighting situation form lighting is basically just standard three-quarter lighting uh rally called it formal lighting because this is the lighting condition that gives us the greatest illusion of three-dimensional form in paint so Riley came up with scales for all different sorts of variations you can see in the basic oh the basic formulating scale that the shadow range is more compressed than the light range so the shadow range ranges from Pure Black to value four so a white object in the shadow would be value four and the light range ranges from Pure White down to the value four so a black object in the light would be value for so you have complete separation between the shadow values and the light values which also gives you that full sentence of three-dimensional form if there's more light because there's more stronger light there's more light in the environment more light bouncing around all the values get compressed towards light if there's less light in the environment all the values get compressed towards dark if there's darker than normal surroundings there's less ambient light so the shadow scale gets compressed if there's lighter than normal surroundings there's more ambient light so the shadow scale moves up and overlaps the white scale in outdoor sunlight because the you have two light sources and the Skylight is lighting up the Shadows you use that Riley lectured a lot and his students took copious notes um so and students often traded notes to fill in gaps and things of things that they hadn't picked up so in that sense Riley's teaching method was preserved in a fairly intact way compared to the teaching of many other influential teachers where we have much much less first-hand information about what they actually taught in the 1970s Binion Smith which is a large Art Supply manufacturer for instance they uh they make Crayola crayons um they introduced what they called modular colors in their permanent pigment oil colors and liquitex acrylic lines um and these were literally an adult straight adaptation of the monsell system to paint with a set of neutral Grays and pure High chroma Hues at specific values um this was essentially taking the principle behind Riley's mixing system um and pre-packaging it um Riley was not mentioned in any of the promotional material the modular colors were not particularly successful commercially so in 1980 they were folded into the regular line of liquitex paints also permanent pigments was retired as a brand name because liquitex had become the more successful brand and so oil paints were also marketed under the liquitex brand a liquid Edge also began including muscle notation on all its paint tubes and they published two products um the color map and mixing guide which was a large poster sized um product that had samples actual paint samples um of both our oil and acrylic line at nine values with the instructions on which paints have been used to mix values that didn't come from the tube and also grade down with the corresponding neutral Ray values they also published a book called How to mix and use color both of these went through several additions and revisions over the course of the 1980s and the 1990s um liquitex produced fewer and fewer values of gray over the years so they originally produced values two through eight um and then they stopped um producing odd numbered values um or maybe even number of values I don't remember which until eventually they only produced value five neutral gray so the later versions of the map and the mixing guide include much much more mixing um in these things however this was a lot of people's introduction to the idea of applying model principles to actual paint mixer yeah so many of Riley students um went into teaching themselves uh Jack Fargo who I mentioned previously continued to teach at the art students League until 2016. um he wrote two books on the Riley um the student's guide to painting that I mentioned previously and a book called mastering drawing a human figure and both of them have been recently reprinted by silver books um John Aviano who was a student of rallies in the 1940s when he was very young taught private lessons for many many years his student grade in Parish has elaborated and refined the application of the Mortal system to painting he teaches color workshops around the country ironically Aviano likes several um abandoned the use of the monsell system in terms of the relationship of the Hues Riley one of the reasons why Rowley adopted monsell was because the compliments in the monsala system are closer to True visual compliments than the traditional red yellow blue color wheel um but many of the students evidently had a hard time um getting their brains around monsell so Aviano um abandoned monsell and applied Riley's system to a more traditional color wheel um John Ennis I'm not uh not giant Ennis uh Doug Higgins um in his book um talks about how he did the same um John Grado in his book um angel agrero in his book talks about how he did the same um so Grayden Parish learned about this approach from Aviano and he has gone completely the other way um so Graden has elaborated and refined the application of the month-old system the painting to the extent where he has tried to find paint matches for the highest Roma colors um in the month so big book of color Nielsen Carlin who was another Aviano student um around the Carlin Academy of Fine Art in Pennsylvania um Neil is the current custodian of Riley's papers so after Riley's death um several of this former students set up a foundation called the Riley Heritage Foundation um to take possession of his uh his notes Riley never had children um it may have been partially why he was so dedicated to teaching um he he had a very very fraternal relationship um with his students that was reciprocated so the Riley Heritage Foundation was dissolved um in the early 2000s um at that point it was let's see guess who who it pretty much consisted of at that point um John Ennis was given custody of Riley's notes and as I said he published uh quite a lot of them on a Blog in about 10 years ago um but the the scope of the project eventually became too much for him um so uh you know Colin now has Riley's papers and he is preparing to post all of them online he has a site um called the Riley notes and he's just working on sort of dotting all the legal eyes and crossing the t's um and then once that is all resolved the goal is to finally pre-republish all of this material um John Frederick Murray taught private lessons on Long Island in New York for many many years um one of his students was Marvin madelson who teaches at the School of Visual Arts in Manhattan Marvin is who I learned the rally material initially from um and Marvin was able to bring John in as a faculty member at SCA where he taught through the 1990s Marvin still teaches his own modified version of the rally method at SCA and in online workshops um Marvin's main modification which is a modification that others have also done similarly used to not start with full chroma colors for the flesh palette because you have to do an awful lot of neutralizing um to get realistic uh complexion colors from those maximum chroma yellows and oranges um so Marvin uses a three strings of Earth colors um when he is painting flesh um so he uses an okra string a orangey red birth string and a purplish red Earth string and when you read how other people have modified the approach this is sort of a recurring theme lots and lots of people have taken the basic idea of using grace to neutralize having nine values to work with um and simply adapted it to pallets that fit their needs better there is a very very active group of people teaching Riley material on the West Coast and they mostly Trace back to a student of Riley's named Fred fixler who studied with Riley in the late 40s um fixer was actually James bauma's roommate at the time when he was studying with Riley um sixer moved to California in the 60s um he worked in publishing he worked in the uh in the entertainment industry um and he taught at several schools in California and eventually he started his own school called the California art institute in 1983 and he was able to attract a group of really really Dynamic students around him um including uh glenorbic Mark westimo uh Andrew forward Hoy Greg Pro um Morgan wiesling a bunch of people who have gone on to be very successful in the entertainment industry and in the gallery world and several of whom also um took over teaching when um ships were retired in the uh in the late 80s one of the students at the California art institute was Jeff Watts who studied briefly with sixler um but mostly with uh with Glenn warbick and those other guys um and he founded the Watts Atelier in Southern California in 1992. um and Watts has been one of the institutions that has been really really successful in keeping this material active and Alive watch students include Eric gist who still who teaches at watts and Stan pokepenko who has his own sort of uh online art education Empire these days an interesting thing about the California lineage is that the instruction tends to focus much more on Riley's drawing program um than the painting program and I think some of this probably traces back to fitzler who didn't seem to be as interested in the painting aspect as the drawing aspect um sixer mostly worked in gouache for one thing and uh and the the system does not kind of correspond as well to gouache although Eric just does Teach an abbreviated version of Riley's palette and watch so the last few things I want to cover are my own uses of the palette so these are photos of my paint cabinet um the Grays the warm colors and the cool colors Riley had his students mix and Tube paints when they got to a certain point I may have taken this to an extreme although most of these are small water watercolor size tubes they are not full Studio sized paint tubes so I will say that in my defense uh here are some pictures of the palette set uh here are several color notes from jobs most of these are fairly old because I tended to do this less and less with experience but we have small color studies and then I would note the uh the mixtures that I used here is a finished painting that there were some questions a while back I I well I know I know we sort of we we filled the time but I'm certainly willing to stick around and answer some questions if uh that people want to there aren't very many um there's one from John Francis um Tristan how do you calibrate your row of colors with the same saturation all right EG do you use a months or book I have but honestly most of it is simply done by eye um Riley's system does not rely on absolute accuracy um especially when it's being used for things like life painting depending on the individual it can be used as a really really rigid framework or can be used as sort of more of a general guide and certainly the values are the most important thing Riley actually said that in terms of painting um value was the value structure was 80 of the painting and then the chroma was 20 and the actual specific Hues really didn't matter that much um this is an extreme position because Riley was definitely not a colorist um he was very much a tonalist painter and a tonalist teacher getting the values correct is the most important thing um and in terms of bringing down them down to uniform chroma honestly most of that is done by eye there are certainly um painters who get into extreme detail trying to be as accurate as they can um and actually matching their colors to um to the monsell book um and trying to identify the local colors of the scene that they are painting with the monsell book um for me that degree has never been necessary for a couple reasons um first of all like Riley I am primarily working for reproduction so there's going to be a bit of variation in terms of what people end up seeing anyway and I'm also mostly inventing color and lighting um I am not transcribing reality in most of my paintings because they are storytelling illustrations in most cases I'm painting things that don't actually exist so what this approach does is it allows me to invent color and Lighting in a more convincing way and that's a big part of of Riley's value scale system was to enable people for instance if you needed to paint a picture in Moonlight um but you had your your models for your painting were shot in a photo studio um it was a way of having sort of a guide into what you had to do to the values to transpose one sort of lighting setup to another and another thing that I didn't really get into is Riley really really gets into the effect of colored light sources and how colored light sources are going to shift colors around inside that color space um so that that super super degree of really really precise Fidelity to observe nature um isn't something that's necessarily built into the original system and it isn't something that is that important to me Riley was actually really really against copying he was really really against strict observation it was all about interpretation um so as I said you know the the color and value range of Nature has to get compressed down to the available value range and chromas that are available in um and he sort of thought about everything in that way um he was a big fan of editing or leaving things out or exaggerating things when they were necessary or useful for the main idea of addiction uh anything else um uh was there one there was something from Kenneth Massey I think oh yeah I see that yes yes so um Kenneth says I recall an online discussion several years ago between Madison and Parish if I recall correctly Madison favored a more limited color space where Parish argued that the size of the color space was pragmatic and depended on lighting conditions um to reference Gurney I imagine madelson had in mind a coach based comparable to gamut Maps um I can't really speak for either of those uh those gentlemen I do know that they have sort of very different ideas and approaches about mixing color um I know that yes Grayden parish is a big fan of getting the widest possible um and highest possible chromos um where as as I discussed earlier um Marvin is actually modified certainly his complexion palette to use a much more a much more lower chroma Earth color based complexion palette and to some degree I certainly see the value in where both of them are coming from it really has to do with the facts that their going for different results in the end um sometimes they're painting different subject matter and I think the value of the general Riley palette approach even though it seems very very rigid is actually how flexible it is as I said the the fundamental concept is bringing colors to the same value before you mix them and neutralizing with Grays instead of with compliments um and those basic principles can actually be applied much more broadly than the specifics of the of this particular might imply that's right yes it's always amazing students are always Blown Away to see how easily they can mix colors when you first do it so well and the other thing the thing that that you know was of the most value to me in learning this approach was being able to internalize a three-dimensional color space to understand how colors move through that color space and to understand that the way that Color Works in terms of is not necessarily an often definitely isn't analogous to the way that it works in pigments um so for instance if you are you know painting a scene under very very bluish light and you have oranges and yellows in your scene um you can't just mix blue into your local colors to get the effect of the light because your yellows are going to go green and your oranges are going to go green whereas if you look how monsell sets things up um your yellows are going to neutralize and your oranges are going to neutralize slightly but also shift towards red oranges and understanding that allows you know certainly allowed me to set up my palette so that I could paint those effects convincingly well I think it's um it's a quarter past uh 12 here in in Sydney so uh they head out and
